name: Santoña Forts and Marshes Loop from Noja
name_original: Los tres fuertes de Santoña pasando por Las Marismas – circular desde Noja
description: Cycle a moderate 22.7-mile loop from Noja, exploring the three forts of Santoña and the bird-rich Las Marismas Natural Park.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Noja offer a diverse landscape for exploration, characterized by its Cantabrian coastline, extensive marshlands, and lush countryside. The region features a mix of mostly paved surfaces, with some routes potentially including unpaved segments. Cyclists can navigate low, rolling valleys and dramatic coastal cliffs, providing varied terrain for different skill levels. Best touring cycling routes around Noja The most popular touring cycling route is Santoña Forts and Marshes Loop from Noja, a 22.7 miles (36.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 25 minutes to complete. This route allows cyclists to discover the forts of Santoña and the rich birdlife of the Las Marismas Natural Park. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is San Martín Fort – Pastelería Cristina loop from Noja, an easy 14.5 miles (23.4 km) path. This ride is suitable for various fitness levels, winding through the local landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Ajo Lighthouse – Playa de Ris loop from Noja, a 11.5 miles (18.5 km) trail leading through coastal scenery with views of Ajo Lighthouse and Playa de Ris, often completed in about 1 hour 16 minutes. Touring cycling around Noja is defined by coastal paths, significant marshlands, and lush countryside. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 140 reviews. More than 1300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Noja's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Noja?
A: Noja offers a diverse selection of over 20 touring cycling routes, catering to various skill levels and interests. These routes explore the region's unique blend of coastline, marshlands, and lush countryside.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Noja?
A: Yes, Noja has several easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40335830" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Martín Fort – Pastelería Cristina loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 23.4 km ride that winds through the local landscape and is suitable for various fitness levels.

Q: Can I find challenging touring cycling routes in Noja?
A: Absolutely. For cyclists seeking a more demanding experience, Noja offers routes with significant elevation gain and longer distances. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40339043" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Venero Tower – Salvador Hedilla Monument loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; is a hard 48 km ride requiring very good fitness, featuring over 570 meters of ascent.

Q: Which routes offer the best scenic views of the coast or natural landscapes?
A: Many routes in Noja boast stunning coastal and natural views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38207591" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ajo Lighthouse – Playa de Ris loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; provides picturesque coastal scenery, including views of Ajo Lighthouse and Playa de Ris. The dramatic cliffs and rugged stretches along the coastline are a highlight of the region.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Noja?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Noja are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012281340/santona-forts-and-marshes-loop-from-noja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña Forts and Marshes Loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3991217" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Ris – Cabo Quejo loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I explore while cycling in Noja?
A: Noja's cycling routes often pass by significant natural and historical landmarks. You can explore the rich birdlife of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park, or cycle past the three forts of Santoña. The coastline also features dramatic cliffs and beautiful beaches like Playa de Ris and Playa de Trengandín. For specific points of interest, consider visiting &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3387497" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brusco (Mijedo)&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2984253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Faro del Pescador&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is Noja a good destination for birdwatching while cycling?
A: Yes, Noja is an excellent destination for birdwatching, especially when combined with cycling. The town is part of the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park, a vital wetland ecosystem and one of northern Spain's most important sites for migratory birds. Cycling routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012281340/santona-forts-and-marshes-loop-from-noja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña Forts and Marshes Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer direct access to these rich birdwatching areas.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Noja?
A: The touring cycling routes around Noja feature a varied terrain. You'll find mostly paved surfaces, ideal for touring bikes, but some routes may include unpaved or more challenging segments. The landscape transitions from low, rolling valleys to dramatic coastal cliffs, offering a diverse cycling experience.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Noja?
A: Noja's mild climate makes it suitable for touring cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds, ideal for exploring the region's natural beauty. Summer can also be enjoyable, especially for coastal rides, though it may be busier.

Q: Are there any 'Greenways' or dedicated cycling paths in the broader Cantabria region accessible from Noja?
A: While Noja itself offers a network of routes, the broader Cantabria region is known for its 'Greenways' (Vías verdes). These are disused railway infrastructures converted into cycling and walking routes, such as the Pas Greenway and Besaya Greenway. These provide extensive, often traffic-free, opportunities for touring cyclists to explore further afield.

Q: What do other cyclists say about the touring cycling experience in Noja?
A: The touring cycling routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 140 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from coastal paths to marshlands, and the variety of routes suitable for different ability levels.

Q: Can I combine a cycling trip with visiting Noja's famous beaches?
A: Absolutely! Noja is renowned for its beautiful, sandy beaches like Playa de Ris and Playa de Trengandín. Many cycling routes offer views of or access to these beaches, allowing you to easily combine a ride with a relaxing stop by the sea. Playa de Trengandín is particularly known for its unique 'moon-like landscape' of karst rock formations at low tide.
tips: A convent currently occupied by the Capuchin Fathers. It was declared a National Monument in 1981. Its origins go back to the Late Middle Ages. In 1597 it was the burial place of Barbara Blomberg, mother of Don Juan of Austria and lover of Emperor Charles V. It is worth making a stop to visit it. It is also a nice place to take some photos of the marsh.

The Natural Park of the Marismas de Santoña, Victoria and Joyel is the largest wetland in Cantabria with an area that covers 6679 hectares. It is the ideal place for bird watching - more than 121 species of waterfowl live here.
Stunning views of Laredo, and a very interesting construction.

Old fortification that defended the town of Santoña from the entry of enemy ships. It is the oldest in Santoña and was built with earth and wood at the beginning of the 17th century. With its 37 guns it was essential for the defense of the port. In 1992 it was declared an Asset of Cultural Interest.

The Fort of Napoleon is smaller than those of San Martín and San Carlos, because although it was thought to be the largest in the area and consisted of different buildings, it was never finished. It was a large solid work, with several buildings, built under the supervision of the Count of Cafarelli, who was the general commander of Napoleon's troops in northern Spain. Some buildings that served as accommodation for officers and soldiers and the Rouget Battery, built in 1811, are still preserved. The guard booth, unique from this period in Cantabria, is also preserved.

faqs.question: What is the estimated cycling time for this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated cycling time for this moderate route is approximately 2 hours and 25 minutes. This duration can vary depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or visit the historical sites.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate, requiring a good level of fitness. The terrain is primarily paved, making it generally suitable for various skill levels. However, be aware that some segments of moderate routes may be unpaved and potentially challenging.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route in Noja?
faqs.answer: While specific parking lots are not detailed, the route is circular and starts from Noja. You can typically find parking in or around the town center of Noja, which serves as a convenient starting point for the loop.

faqs.question: What historical sites and natural attractions can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in both natural beauty and history. You'll pass through the stunning Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park, known for its diverse birdlife. Key historical highlights include the three forts: San Martín Fort, Fuerte de San Carlos, and Fuerte de Napoleón. You'll also see the Monastery of San Sebastián de Montehano, the Casa de las Mareas, and the 16th-century Molino de Santa Olaja (an old tide mill).

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The route passes through the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park. While dogs are generally permitted in natural parks, it's crucial to keep them on a leash to protect the diverse birdlife and other wildlife, especially in wetland areas. Always ensure your dog is under control and respect local regulations.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required to cycle through the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park. However, as a protected area, it's important to respect all environmental regulations, stay on marked paths, and avoid disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) months. During these periods, the weather is usually mild and pleasant for cycling, and you can enjoy optimal birdwatching opportunities in the marshes. Summer can be warm, and winter might bring cooler, wetter conditions.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I observe in Las Marismas Natural Park?
faqs.answer: The Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park is a vital wetland complex, renowned for its diverse birdlife. You can expect to see over 130 identified species, making it an excellent spot for birdwatching. Keep an eye out for various migratory and resident birds, especially during spring and autumn.

faqs.question: Does this cycling route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Northern Route (Cantabria), the Senda del Litoral, and the Monte Buciero – Faros y Acantilados trail, among others, adding variety to your journey.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options along the route or in Noja/Santoña?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Noja and Santoña are popular coastal towns with a good selection of cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. You'll find places to refuel and relax before, during, or after your ride, especially in the main urban areas.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this touring cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and length, it's advisable to bring plenty of water, snacks, and appropriate cycling gear. Sun protection (sunscreen, hat, sunglasses) is recommended, especially in open marsh areas. A basic bike repair kit and a first-aid kit are also good to have. Don't forget binoculars for birdwatching!
